title: What is the purpose of the Tree of Life
display_title: What is the purpose of the Tree of Life
wikitext:
[]: What is the purpose of the Tree of Life?             Quote Post by flatulant » Mon Jan 01, 2007 10:23 am This is something else that has been bothering me. It seems to have no purpose, but if that were the case, they wouldn't have kept it in there. Help!

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Jan 01, 2007 10:32 am Which Tree of Life? The one that the harpies form with EVA-01, or the one that Yui-sama makes with the Spear of Longinus?

[]:             Quote Post by MaggotMaster » Mon Jan 01, 2007 10:34 am Both are there to look cool, man. when will people understand that the mechanics of 3I aren't supposed to make any sense.

[]:             Quote Post by flatulant » Mon Jan 01, 2007 10:35 am Wait, there are two? I thought that there was only 1, formed near the beginning of 26'. Edit: Nevermind, the one with the LnY and Eva Shogouki. Edit 2: Actually, both look useless.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon Jan 01, 2007 11:21 am The Sephirotic Tree of Life has thematic relevance -- some of it kinda vague -- but it's a real bitch to explain to anyone not somehow acquainted with Kabbalah. (And even then, it's still convoluted as hell.) The EVA-01 Tree of Life is, apparently, a more straightforward representation of the Cosmic Tree (than the SToL), but, if I was ever able to make an iota of sense out of it, I promptly forgot everything. But seriously. "Fruit of life, fruit of knowledge", "she who possesses them both is restored to the Tree of Life", "embryo of lives", "ark or demon"... hawt kernficken, a myriad eyes that open with "sounds like praying voices"... Don't tell me that doesn't make PER-fect sense!

[]:             Quote Post by felineki » Mon Jan 01, 2007 12:06 pm Reichu wrote: a myriad eyes that open with a sound "like praying voices"...

[]:             Quote Post by Rommel » Tue Jan 02, 2007 4:58 pm IIRC, in Kabalistic tradition, the Tree of Life is the tree God gave to the Angels. In essense, it is eternal life. The Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil is the tree that was planted in the Garden of Eden, which man was fogiven to eat from, and from which he did eat. The difference here is that the Tree of Knowledge, while not giving you eternal life, gave man the ability to think for itself. The decition to disobey God is man's expresion of Free Will, something that is beyond even the Angels, and something that makes man basically like a God. Genesis wrote: And the LORD God said, Behold, the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil: and now, lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever: Therefore the LORD God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken. So he drove out the man; and he placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims[Angels], and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Tue Jan 02, 2007 5:16 pm Psst, NGE isn't a religious show. All that crap was added to make it look cool. Oh and to give fan-wankers a major headache.

[]:             Quote Post by jakewashere » Tue Jan 02, 2007 5:18 pm Animals (of which there are notably few in NGE) are beneath good and evil, existing purely on instinct. if I can speak of Judeochristian mythology in an extremely simplified and technical sense (as NGE itself does): When Adam and Eve took the Fruit of Knowledge, they were raised up to a higher level of sentience. They achieved self-awareness. They realized that they had free will, and - as God had warned them - they realized all the other unpleasant things that come from being sentient and self-aware (things much in evidence when one watches the series). There was no going back to the innocence of animals; the human race was doomed to be self-aware. To the extent that animals do think, it is only on the level of survival: where one's next meal is coming from, where the opportunites to reproduce are, how to defend oneself against physical threat. It is our autonomy, physical and mental, that makes us higher than animals; instead of merely being in the world, we are aware of being in the world, and capable of pondering it on a level above "How am I going to survive?" Our autonomy, therefore, also causes a great deal of our emotional pain. And I'm not sure why I have to tell you any of this, since we're all Eva fans here and we're familiar with the idea.

[]:             Quote Post by cat42 » Tue Jan 02, 2007 6:22 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: Psst, NGE isn't a religious show. All that crap was added to make it look cool. Oh and to give fan-wankers a major headache.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Tue Jan 02, 2007 6:40 pm cat42 wrote: Anonymous_Evafan wrote: Psst, NGE isn't a religious show. All that crap was added to make it look cool. Oh and to give fan-wankers a major headache.

[]:             Quote Post by Shwiggie » Wed Jan 03, 2007 12:59 am Who says the show has Christian meaning, anyway? No more than, say, Xenogears or what-have-you. And it's a good thing it wasn't intended to...otherwise I'd probably consider it sacriledge :) However, the religious imagery is quite rich, providing a whole layer of depth to the final product, whether it was originally intended or not. The caveat to the whole thing being not to read too much into so-called deeper meanings. And with the quote of Tsurumaki regarding the choice of giving it more meaning, they may just do that with the upcoming revisitation projects. So it may be more than plot-dressing in the near future.

[]:             Quote Post by Rommel » Wed Jan 03, 2007 2:19 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: Psst, NGE isn't a religious show. All that crap was added to make it look cool. Oh and to give fan-wankers a major headache.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Jan 03, 2007 2:52 pm Rommel wrote: Hell, the Tree of Life, the Sephirot, is a Jewish symbol and has nothing to do with Christianity as we know it today.

[]:             Quote Post by Rommel » Wed Jan 03, 2007 3:02 pm I meant to use 'Sephirot' to indicate the sketch or diagram of the tree, as opposed to the metaphysical 'idea' of the tree, which was the point in quoting Genesis.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Jan 04, 2007 2:35 pm Rommel wrote: I meant to use 'Sephirot' to indicate the sketch or diagram of the tree, as opposed to the metaphysical 'idea' of the tree, which was the point in quoting Genesis.
